narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 22 (well under the usual 36.70), Year 1 school decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 1 (compared to 5.53), middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans—and predicted a 63% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try. This high failure risk led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 academic decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile—placing you in the bottom tier of your school's grades that year—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling a concerning drop in performance over time. Your LSAT score reinforced this, as its below-average level further increased the predicted risk. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile, while not top-tier, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success compared to what it might have been otherwise. Overall, these elements made your profile riskier than typical admitted students, who tend to show stronger, consistent academic climbs and higher standardized scores. Many peers in similar situations, especially from underrepresented backgrounds like yours, have turned things around with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and exploring post-baccalaureate options or community college bridges that have helped others rebuild their records. Schools often value resilience, so highlighting any upward trends or personal growth in future applications could make a real difference. With focused effort, you can shift this prediction in your favor.
